source_paper_title: On the Equivalence of Zero-Sum Games and Conic Programs
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Dimou_2026, title={On the Equivalence of Zero-Sum Games and Conic Programs}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2025.0926}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2025.0926}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Dimou, Nikos}, year={2026}, month=mar }
context:
Section 7 (Future directions), page 34.

After proving that every two-player zero-sum game with cone-leveled strategy sets can be described by a primal-dual pair of conic linear programs, the following question naturally arises: When is a two-player zero-sum game equilibrium-equivalent to a game with cone-leveled strategy sets?
title: Equilibrium-equivalent reduction of zero-sum games to cone-leveled strategy games
background_latex:
A two-player zero-sum game is a triple \(G=(S,T,u)\), where \(S\subseteq X\) and \(T\subseteq Y\) are the strategy sets of players I and II, and \(u:S\times T\to\mathbb{R}\) is the payoff to player I.

In the setting considered here, \(X\) and \(Y\) are (reflexive) Banach spaces with continuous duals \(X^*\) and \(Y^*\). A bilinear pairing is denoted by \(\langle \cdot,\cdot\rangle\). A (closed convex) cone \(C\subseteq X\) has positive dual cone \(C^*:=\{w\in X^*: \langle w,x\rangle\ge 0\ \forall x\in C\}\), and similarly for a cone \(K\subseteq Y\).

A cone-leveled set is a subset \(S\subseteq X\) that can be written as
\[
S=\{x\in C: \langle \alpha,x\rangle\in H\},
\]
for some convex cone \(C\subseteq X\), some nonzero functional \(\alpha\in X^*\), and some nonempty set \(H\subseteq (0,\infty)\) (typically an interval). A basic cone-leveled set is the special case \(H=\{p\}\) (a single level). A base of a cone is a basic cone-leveled set \(\{x\in C: \langle \alpha,x\rangle=1\}\) with \(\alpha\in \mathrm{int}(C^*)\).

Two games \(G=(S,T,u)\) and \(G'=(S',T',u')\) are equilibrium-equivalent if they have the same set of Nash equilibria after an identification of strategies (e.g., via maps between \(S\) and \(S'\), \(T\) and \(T'\)) that preserves saddle-point inequalities, so that equilibria and game values correspond under the identification.

The paper establishes that games whose strategy sets are cone-leveled (in particular, bases of convex cones) and whose payoff is bilinear \(u(x,y)=\langle y,Ax\rangle\) can be analyzed/solved via associated conic linear programs and duality. This motivates asking which more general games can be reduced, without changing equilibrium structure, to this cone-leveled class.
problem_definition_latex:
Characterize (or give verifiable necessary and sufficient conditions for) when a given two-player zero-sum game \(G=(S,T,u)\) is equilibrium-equivalent to a two-player zero-sum game \(\widetilde G=(\widetilde S,\widetilde T,\widetilde u)\) with cone-leveled strategy sets \(\widetilde S\subseteq \widetilde X\), \(\widetilde T\subseteq \widetilde Y\) and bilinear payoff of the form
\[
\widetilde u(\tilde x,\tilde y)=\langle \tilde y, \widetilde A\tilde x\rangle
\]
for some linear operator \(\widetilde A\), such that Nash equilibria (saddle points) of \(G\) correspond to Nash equilibria of \(\widetilde G\) under the equivalence mapping.

Characterize (or give verifiable necessary and sufficient conditions for) when a given two-player zero-sum game \(G=(S,T,u)\) is equilibrium-equivalent to a two-player zero-sum game \(\widetilde G=(\widetilde S,\widetilde T,\widetilde u)\) with cone-leveled strategy sets \(\widetilde S\subseteq \widetilde X\), \(\widetilde T\subseteq \widetilde Y\) and bilinear payoff of the form
\[
\widetilde u(\tilde x,\tilde y)=\langle \tilde y, \widetilde A\tilde x\rangle
\]
for some linear operator \(\widetilde A\), such that Nash equilibria (saddle points) of \(G\) correspond to Nash equilibria of \(\widetilde G\) under the equivalence mapping.
open_problem_quote: After proving that every two-player zero-sum game with cone-leveled strategy sets can be described by a primal-dual pair of conic linear programs, the following question naturally arises: When is a two-player zero-sum game equilibrium-equivalent to a game with cone-leveled strategy sets?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a general characterization of when an arbitrary game can be reduced (via equilibrium-preserving transformations) to the cone-leveled/bilinear class. There is no obvious finite-dimensional, efficiently checkable search space for counterexamples without first formalizing what class of equivalence mappings is allowed.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: If one fixes a concrete notion of equilibrium-equivalence (e.g., affine/linear embeddings of mixed-strategy extensions) and restricts to finite-dimensional games, an automated loop could search for embeddings into cone-leveled representations and score candidates by whether equilibria map correctly (computable via LP/SDP). However, in the general infinite-dimensional setting and without a specified equivalence notion, the search space and fitness signal are underspecified.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Dimou’s framework isolates a broad class of zero-sum games—those with cone-leveled strategy sets and bilinear payoff \,\(u(x,y)=\langle y,Ax\rangle\)—for which equilibria and values can be computed via associated conic linear programs, and conversely shows an “almost equivalence” between minimax and strong duality in reflexive Banach spaces. The open problem asks for a characterization of when an arbitrary zero-sum game \(G=(S,T,u)\) is equilibrium-equivalent (via strategy identifications preserving saddle inequalities) to such a cone-leveled bilinear game \(\widetilde G\), thereby enabling conic-programming machinery to solve the original game without changing its equilibrium structure.

The only forward-citing work provided here makes partial progress in a major subclass: semidefinite programming. It constructs, under verifiable constraint qualifications (strong optimality or strict unboundedness), an equilibrium-preserving reduction from an SDP pair to a zero-sum semidefinite game whose strategy sets are density matrices (bases of the PSD cone) and whose payoff is bilinear. This aligns closely with Dimou’s cone-base setting and demonstrates that, at least for SDPs, one can build explicit equivalence mappings that allow recovery of primal/dual solutions or certificates from game equilibria.

However, the general characterization problem remains open: beyond specific conic-programming-derived games (LP/SDP and variants), there is no known necessary-and-sufficient condition describing exactly which general Banach-space games admit an equilibrium-equivalent embedding into the cone-leveled bilinear class. Promising directions suggested by Dimou’s paper include identifying invariants of equilibrium structure preserved under positive-homogeneous rescalings and cone-base normalizations, and developing criteria that recognize when a game’s payoff and feasible strategy geometry can be represented (possibly after lifting) as linear images of cone slices (bases or cone-leveled sets), as in moment/SOS liftings for polynomial games.

sources.title: On the equivalence of semidefinite programming and zero-sum semidefinite games
sources.source_url: (source URL not provided in forward-citation analysis)
sources.theorem_or_result:
Under a constraint qualification for an SDP pair \((P,D)\)—either (i) existence of strongly optimal primal-dual solutions (attainment and zero duality gap) or (ii) existence of a strictly unbounded direction—the paper constructs a modified zero-sum semidefinite game \(G_M\) (players’ strategies are density matrices with a block-diagonal form encoding \((X,y,t)\) plus an extra scalar \(u\)) with bilinear payoff \(p(\bar X,\bar Y)\) such that:
- \(\mathrm{val}(G_M)=0\) iff a strongly optimal primal-dual pair exists; and if \(\mathrm{val}(G_M)=0\), any optimal strategy of player 2 has \(t>0\) and yields strongly optimal SDP solutions \((X/t,\,y/t)\) (Theorem 3.6).
- If a strictly unbounded direction exists, then for any optimal strategy of player 1 one has \(t'=0\) and the strategy yields an unbounded direction certificate for \(P\) or \(D\) (Theorem 3.10).
Moreover, for rational input they give an explicit bound \(M\) (via KKT root/coordinate bounds) guaranteeing the reduction works in the bounded case (Corollary 4.3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for necessary/sufficient conditions for when a general zero-sum game \((S,T,u)\) is equilibrium-equivalent to a cone-leveled, bilinear game \(\langle \tilde y,\tilde A\tilde x\rangle\). This paper provides such an equilibrium-preserving reduction in one important subclass: SDPs (a specific conic program over \(S_+^n\)) are reduced to a zero-sum semidefinite game whose strategy sets are density matrices (a base of the PSD cone, hence basic cone-leveled) and whose payoff is bilinear in the players’ matrix strategies. The reduction is shown to preserve optimal strategies/equilibria and to allow recovery of primal/dual SDP solutions or infeasibility/unboundedness certificates, but only under a stated constraint qualification (strong optimality or strict unboundedness). It does not characterize reductions for arbitrary Banach-space games \((S,T,u)\), nor for general cones beyond the semidefinite cone, though it suggests extension to conic games as future work.
